lessonTitle: Pregnancy Optimization in Sickle Cell Disease

slides.title: Learning Objectives

items.html: Appreciate the maternal, fetal and pregnancy risks for pregnant people with sickle cell disease
Review prepregnancy optimization and management during pregnancy

slides.title: Pregnancy Risks in Sickle Cell Disease

data.bubble_text: Pregnancy in SCD is associated with increased pulmonary complications, infections, antepartum bleeding, incidence of pain episodes, venous thromboembolism (VTE), and hospitalization. People with SCD are also at an increased risk of pre-eclampsia and fetal growth restriction.

slides.title: Lived Experience Advocate: Ms. Thea Campbell

data.html: Thea wanted to take the opportunity to share her experiences with being pregnant and some of the challenges she faced, which may help you when you are caring for a person wanting to conceive or a pregnant person with SCD. Note: Thea refers to her disease as "being a carrier to Sickle Cell Anemia", but she does have Sickle Cell Disease and is not a Carrier who would have Sickle Cell Trait.

slides.title: Pre-Pregnancy Preparation

items.html: How Can You Best Prepare Your Patient for Pregnancy?
Optimizing health prior to conception can significantly reduce the risk of pregnancy morbidity and mortality. Also, maintain folic acid supplementation.

Conduct screenings for: HIV, Hepatitis B + C, Thyroid disease, Kidney disease, Retinopathy, Cardiac Function, Pulmonary Hypertension, Osteopenia (bone density), Silent infarcts (head MRI), Liver iron deposition (liver MRI)
Ensure recommended vaccinations are up to date: Meningococcus, Pneumococcus, Haemophilus influenza B, Influenza, Hepatitis B, Rubella, Varicella, COVID 19

Counsel on the potential harms on fertility and pregnancy of: Alcohol, Cannabis, Smoking, Drug use. Counsel on benefits of therapies for pain including: Massage, Warm heating pads, Mind body techniques

slides.title: Optimizing Organ Function and Reducing Drug Exposures Before Pregnancy

cells.testing: ALT; Ferritin; Abdominal ultrasound; MRI for hepatic iron concentration
Echocardiogram; Cardiac MRI with T2* for cardiac iron assessment (if history of hepatic iron overload)

slides.title: Drag the organs on the left to the appropriate test on the right (done pre pregnancy)

items.html: Cardiac: Echocardiogram; Cardiac MRI with T2* for cardiac iron assessment
Neurological: MRI brain
Renal: Creatinine; Assessment of proteinuria

slides.title: Optimizing Organ Function and Reducing Drug Exposures Before Pregnancy (Continued)

items.html: Multidisciplinary Team: Pregnant women with SCD must be cared for by a high risk obstetrical team knowledgeable in SCD and include a multidisciplinary team: Maternal Fetal Medicine/Obstetrics, Haematology, Anaesthesia, Neonatology. Referrals are ideally sent prior to pregnancy, but if pregnant as soon as pregnancy is confirmed

Medication Management: Discontinue iron chelation (deferasirox, etc.) Note: for critical iron overload, deferoxamine can be used safely in the second and third trimesters, and while breastfeeding. Discontinue hydroxyurea (research supports consideration of hydroxyurea on an individualized basis). Discontinue NSAIDS, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers. Add prenatal vitamins without iron for those with iron overload. Add Folic Acid 5mg, to help replace folate lost during hemolysis. Ensure individual has drug insurance for costly medications such as LMWH

Other Considerations: Phenotype and genotype of red cell antigens including RhD. Blood group and antibody screen. Dental hygiene. Ensure stability of health conditions to optimize chance of healthy live birth

slides.title: Maternal and Fetal Risks in Pregnancy

data.bubble_text: Maternal and fetal risks in pregnancy for woman with sickle cell disease is what we will review next.

images.alt: Pregnancy is associated with increased maternal and fetal risk.
images.caption: Pregnancy is associated with increased maternal and fetal risk.

slides.title: Maternal/Fetal Risks in Pregnancy: Preterm Preeclampsia

items.html: Preterm Preeclampsia: 12% of patients with SCD develop pre-eclampsia
Maternal Manifestations: Hypertension, Proteinuria, Other end organ dysfunction
Fetal Manifestations: intrauterine growth restriction, preterm birth, oligohydramnios, stillbirth, placental abruption
Preeclampsia Prevention: ASA 150-162 mg every evening. Initiate before 16 weeks' gestation to decrease risk of preeclampsia

slides.title: Maternal/Fetal Risks in Pregnancy: Cardiac Iron Overload

items.html: Prevalence: Happens less frequently than in beta thalassemia: 2.5%
Ferritin and LIC: Small case series suggest the following as indicative of iron overload: Ferritin > 4600 ug/L; LIC > 22 mg/g (mean 44 mg/g)
Risks: Earlier and more frequent transfusion increases risk
Management: Cardiac MRI T2* in the 2nd trimester and consideration of deferoxamine if iron overload

slides.title: Maternal/Fetal Risks in Pregnancy: Pulmonary Complications

items.html: Manifestations: Restrictive lung disease, airway hyper-responsiveness, pulmonary hypertension, and acute chest syndrome
Small airway closure
FEV1 < 65 may predict risk of fetal death: OR 7.81, 95% CI 1.59–38.38, p = 0.01 (Hayfron-Benjamin, 2021)
Management: Pulse oximetry at each visit. Consider home oxygen support in setting of hypoxia

slides.title: Acute Chest Syndrome In Pregnancy

items.html: Predominantly in the 3rd trimester and 4 days postpartum. Most frequent cause of ICU admissions and maternal mortality
Typically preceded by pain
Treatment with transfusion may be warranted in consult with a Haematologist

slides.title: Pain Management in Pregnancy

data.html:
The following can be administered chronically for pain during pregnancy: Acetaminophen, Morphine, Hydromorphone. Chronic use of NSAIDs is discouraged at any point in pregnancy, as it is associated with fetal renal compromise and oligohydramnios. It is contraindicated after 28 weeks' gestation given risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus.

Neonatal abstinence syndrome (NAS)

  • Results from the abrupt discontinuation of opioids at birth after a fetus has become physically dependent
  • Opioid induced physical dependence is a transient phenomenon and treated supportively. It is important to emphasize that optimal treatment of maternal pain (often achieved only with use of opioids) is paramount to avoid ACS and severe maternal morbidity, which can in turn increase fetal risk of adverse events
  • Opioids with slow clearance rates (buprenorphine) have less severe withdrawal vs intermediate (methadone) or fast (morphine) rates
  • In a study by Volkow (2016), 131 patients with SCD were using opioids and 4% had NAS

slides.title: Asymptomatic UTIs

before.heading: Asymptomatic UTIs
before.text: Common in pregnancy and can trigger a pain crisis

after.heading: Risks and Management
after.text: Increases risk of pyelonephritis and in turn pre term labour. Do monthly urine test.

slides.title: Transfusions in Pregnancy in Sickle Cell Disease

data.html: Transfusion for SCD-related complications should only be considered after discussion with haematology and careful reflection on the balance of benefits and risks such as hyperhemolysis, delayed transfusion reactions, and alloimmunization
This chart depicts hemoglobin levels for non pregnant and pregnant patients with SCD. Note the lower hemoglobin levels in pregnant patients.

images.alt: Hemoglobin levels in pregnant vs non-pregnant patients with SCD

data.html:
It is imperative that donor blood undergoes prophylactic red cell antigen matching for Rh (C/c, E/e) and K antigens

Prophylactic transfusion can be considered for specific indications, after discussion with the Haematologist and MFM.

slides.title: What is Hyperhemolysis?

items.html: The destruction of both transfused and autologous RBCs
Can be immediate or delayed
Accompanied by reticulocytopenia, worsening anemia
Can induce multiorgan failure and death most likely due to damage to the underlying vasculature by released free Hb and heme
Further transfusion increases risk of recurrence!
Medications used in pregnancy for treatment include: Rituxan, Eculizumab, Erythropoietin, Toculizumab, Prednisone

slides.title: Identifying Risk for Delayed Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions (DHTR) in Pregnancy

data.html:
• Transfusion during pro-inflammatory events increases the risk of alloimmunization
  • Individuals with low number of previous transfusions or previous delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions are at high risk for DHTR - consider extended matching for Rh, K, Kidd, Duffy, and MNS
  • Jk, Ss, Fy Abs associated with a high risk of subsequent alloimmunization - extended matched advised
  • Autoantibodies, natural antibodies (anti-M, anti-Lewis) associated with low risk of subsequent immunization - CEK matched

When total hemoglobin falls below pre-transfusion levels, suggests an accompanying fall in patient's own cells, indicating hyperhemolysis. Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction in sickle cell indicated by rapid fall in HgbA cells.

slides.title: Fetal Monitoring

items.html: Fetal Exposure to Potentially Teratogenic Medications: If exposure to teratogenic medication has taken place in the 1st trimester, consider the following ultrasounds at: Early anatomy ultrasound (~15 weeks' gestation); Level II anatomy ultrasound (~18-20 weeks' gestation)

Placental Assessment: Given a number of adverse pregnancy outcomes are placentally mediated, consider the following testing: Placental biochemistry e.g. (PAPP-A in first trimester, PlGF around 23 weeks' gestation), if available; Ultrasound evaluation of the placenta including uterine artery Doppler. In the late second and third trimesters, serial ultrasounds for fetal growth and well being should be performed: Estimated fetal weight; Assessment of fetal umbilical artery Doppler; Amniotic fluid volume; Biophysical profile

slides.title: Summary

items.html: Pregnancy is associated with the potential for severe maternal, fetal and pregnancy adverse events
Prepregnancy counselling is essential to reduce those risks by educating about planning a pregnancy, discontinuing potentially teratogenic medications, optimizing organ function
Multidisciplinary management once pregnancy is confirmed includes a Haematologist/MFM (or Obstetrician with expertise in SCD in pregnancy), Anaesthesia, and Paediatrics near birth
Regular maternal and frequent fetal monitoring during pregnancy is necessary to reduce risks
Transfusion during pregnancy is individualized, requires consultation with a Haematologist, and balances risks vs. benefits
